pageTitle.value: Steve Boyes' Great Spine of Africa - Perpetual Planet

WaPageTitle.value: Steve Boyes' Great Spine of Africa - Perpetual Planet

metadescription.value: Explorer Steve Boyes and his team have proven that the Angolan Highlands are the source of the great Zambezi River. Learn more on rolex.org.

text.value: After many perilous expeditions, explorer Steve Boyes and his team have proven that the Angolan Highlands are the source of the great Zambezi River. With Rolex’s support, he is using these findings to secure real protections for this vital area.

At the Ramsar Convention on wetlands in Zimbabwe, Steve Boyes, the founder of the Great Spine of Africa series of expeditions, stood in front of kings, dignitaries and global leaders to share an incredible finding: the primary source of the Zambezi River, which sustains millions of people and extraordinary wildlife, is in the Angolan Highlands. There, mountainous wetlands known as “water towers” act as sponges that feed trickling streams, which eventually converge into the rushing torrents of the Zambezi River. The finding is the culmination of more than 50 gruelling expeditions traversing thousands of kilometres of previously uncharted waterways, tracing the Zambezi back to its very beginnings.

Presenting their work to royalty from traditional kingdoms through which Boyes and his team had travelled was an important moment; the support of the kings has been crucial to the entire project. “These are river guardians,” says Boyes. “They’re still proud of the water. They still feel ownership of the water. So, getting their reaction was just great.”

The 2025 Ramsar Convention took place in the shadow of the Zambezi River’s Victoria Falls, which receives more than 70 per cent of its water from the Angola Highlands. It brought together delegates from around the world – including Nyambe Nyambe, Executive Director of the Kavango Zambezi Transfrontier Conservation Area (KAZA TFCA) – to recognize and conserve critically important wetlands.

The area that Boyes and his team have scientifically demonstrated to hold the Zambezi River’s water sources is called Lisima Lya Mwono (“Source of Life”) and situated in the eastern Angolan Highlands; it has been submitted for recognition as an official Ramsar Site. “This will be the third-largest Ramsar Site in Africa, the fifth largest in the world if it’s listed in the next few months,” explains Boyes.

When the Lisima Lya Mwono Ramsar Site submission is finalized, it will officially be recognized as a wetland of international importance. With 172 national governments participating in the Ramsar Convention – headed up by Secretary General Musonda Mumba – the area would be protected by national and international conservation frameworks, with a particular focus on sustainable use and land management.

Boyes and his team have achieved an awe-inspiring feat – taking years of challenging and often treacherous expeditions, performing meticulous research along the way, to preserve a landscape bigger than England.

title.value: “What I’m most proud of is the connection with the communities and the trust they have in the project.”

text.value:
Building on the success of the National Geographic Okavango Wilderness Project, in 2018 Boyes formed The Wilderness Project to study and safeguard Africa’s major river basins. Four years later, with the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, he launched his young organization’s most ambitious venture yet, the Great Spine of Africa series of expeditions. The undertakings are providing the first scientific baselines of Africa’s major water towers, (areas that act as natural water stores) and investigating their role as the sources of the Zambezi, Congo, Niger and Nile rivers, on which two-thirds of Africa’s economy depend.

Kerllen Costa, Boyes’ expedition partner, played a vital role by forging close relationships with local people who will help protect the region in the decades to come. “What I’m most proud of is the connection with the communities and the trust they have in the project,” says Costa.

Their first Great Spine of Africa mission was the Lungwevungu Expedition, which explored the remote Angolan Highlands. The water towers here were already acknowledged by indigenous people as the source of many of Africa’s great rivers, but had not yet been scientifically documented. As they travelled along the waterways, Boyes, Costa and the team took measurements of the rivers and wildlife to create baselines of the ecosystems. While critical for recording the health and biodiversity of the region, these expeditions are not only dangerous, with crocodiles and hippopotamuses to contend with, but mentally and physically draining. The end of each expedition, as they finally reach the finish point along the river, is a moment for celebration. As Boyes tells it, “You’ve been out there for several months and you see that landing, and you stop, and think, ‘there we go’, lift the trophy and hug each other.”

For Boyes in particular, who was diagnosed with cancer just a few years ago and had to take time out to recover, being back exploring is a special experience. Now, he feels stronger than ever, and has more drive, purpose and energy to succeed in his mission. By collating the information and knowledge from across the project, Boyes and Costa have been able to achieve an impressive goal; to track how the wetlands and rivers are changing, so that they can be better protected.

title.value: “We want to protect 1.2 million square kilometres of African watershed. We want to do that by 2035, perhaps 2030 if we do things right.”

text.value:
Boyes and the team have published a paper describing their pioneering assessment of the Zambezi’s source waters, building on more than a decade of work demonstrating the regional importance of the Angolan Highlands not only to the river’s main drainage basin, but to its sub-basins as well. It is the source of key tributaries feeding the Okavango and Congo. In all, some 400 million people and two-thirds of Africa’s remaining elephant population are dependent on the area that Boyes and Costa are working to conserve, alongside local communities and leaders.

“We want to protect 1.2 million square kilometres of African watershed. We want to do that by 2035, perhaps 2030 if we do things right.” Achieving official recognition and protection as a Ramsar Site for Lisima Lya Mwono will be a massive step towards this goal. Boyes is confident that with greater protections for this area, whole countries will be more resilient in the face of climate change, safeguarding their water and food security.

The team’s work will create a better future for those who depend on the health of the Angolan Highlands. “The legacy of scientists, river guardians and river storytellers that we are leaving behind in these countries, and supporting in these countries, is going to grow into a massive endowment that’s going to support this work into the future,” says Boyes.

Presenting at the Ramsar Convention is a major milestone for Boyes and his Great Spine of Africa team, demonstrating the huge efforts made over the past decade. But as Costa puts it, “I think it's just the beginning.”

pageTitle.value: Steve Boyes and the Cassai River Expedition - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: As part of his Great Spine of Africa Expeditions to protect the continent’s water security, Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative partner and conservationist Steve Boyes has traced a major source of the Congo River back to the Angolan Highlands.

WaPageTitle.value: Steve Boyes and the Cassai River Expedition - Perpetual Planet

metadescription.value: As part of his Great Spine of Africa Expeditions, conservationist Steve Boyes studied the Cassai River, a major source of the Congo River. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Steve Boyes and the Lungwevungu River - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Through its Perpetual Planet Initiative, Rolex supports the Great Spine of Africa expeditions that traverse thousands of kilometres of rivers never scientifically documented before. The research will help protect the communities and wildlife that depend on Africa’s great rivers.

WaPageTitle.value: The mighty Zambezi River and its secrets

metadescription.value: Through its Perpetual Planet Initiative, Rolex supports Steve Boyes in his expedition to study the unexplored Lungwevungu River. More on rolex.org.
